abstract: Specify the secrets your app requires for device management features, receive secrets from MDM servers and use secrets in your app.
overview:
## Overview

Some device management features rely on *secrets*, that is, secure credentials provisioned by an administrator (MDM admin) for your app, such as passwords, certificates, and identities. Secrets can support:

Your app accesses secrets by their identifier using the framework supplied secret *provider* classes. Each provider offers a list of identifiers that catalogs all the currently available secrets, of a particular type, that an MDM admin provisions. You can access secrets using predefined identifiers, or identifiers that you add to your app’s configuration.

The provider’s list of identifiers is an [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/AsyncSequence]. If your app requires notification when the available secrets change, or if your app supports dynamic lists of secrets, iterate the sequence for the lifetime of the app using `for await`.

## Specify the secrets your app supports

To implement features with secrets, identify the provisions that you require from MDM admins. Publish a specification that details the requirements in a location accessible to MDM admins; for example, hosting it on your app website.

When an admin provisions secrets on their MDM server according to your specification, they use [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/DeviceManagement]. The device’s operating system works with [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/DeviceManagement] to ingest the secrets on the device. From the perspective of your app, [doc://com.apple.managedapp/documentation/ManagedApp] receives the secrets automatically.

## Retrieve secrets using a known identifier

The framework defines a provider class for each type of secret:

Another task that often uses known identifiers is responding to client certificate authentication challenges. The following example responds to a client certificate challenge within the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Foundation/URLSessionDelegate] callback, and requests an identity using the [doc://com.apple.managedapp/documentation/ManagedApp/ManagedAppIdentitiesProvider] accessor:

## Retrieve secrets using the provider’s identifiers sequence

If your app needs a notification when the available secrets change, or if your app maintains dynamic lists of secrets, use the provider’s [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Swift/AsyncSequence].  Use a `for await` construct on the provider’s `identifiers` property to listen for changes. The following example builds a list of available usernames by iterating the  [doc://com.apple.managedapp/documentation/ManagedApp/ManagedAppIdentitiesProvider] sequence:

## Access an identity for authentication or signing

To log someone in, an app needs to authenticate with a server. To acquire the person’s identity to authenticate, pass the person’s username as the identifier argument of the identity provider’s accessor, for example:

Alternatively, your app can use the identity for cryptographic signing. Because [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Security/SecIdentity] is in the [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Security] framework, you can create a cryptographic signature using other [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Security] framework API. The following code begins a signature by acquiring a reference to the identity’s private key:

The [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Security/SecKey] object is an opaque reference to the underlying private key data that’s in system memory. Don’t load actual private key data in your app’s memory unless absolutely necessary; for example, when using the  [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Security/SecKeyCopyExternalRepresentation(_:_:)] function.

While choosing the signing algorithm, call [doc://com.apple.documentation/documentation/Security/SecKeyIsAlgorithmSupported(_:_:_:)] to ensure the admin-provisioned key supports the algorithm:

To facilitate compatibility, document the algorithms and associated key types your app supports in your specification.

Finally, create the signature:

items.description: A class that provides certificates that an MDM admin provisions for a managed app or extension.
items.name: ManagedAppCertificatesProvider

items.description: A class that provides identities that an MDM admin provisions for a managed app or extension.
items.name: ManagedAppIdentitiesProvider

items.description: A class that provides passwords that an MDM admin provisions for a managed app or extension.
items.name: ManagedAppPasswordsProvider

title: Accessing provisioned secrets with identifiers
